title: practical english usage - Part 303
content:
Note that when a bit and a little are used with non-comparative adjectives, Can you drive a bit slower? Wait a bit.

She's a bit old to play with dolls, isn't she?

A bit is often used as an adverb with the same meaning as a little (►   168).

406 (a) bit    ________ It's my birthday on Tuesday. Come and help me celebrate.

born. A  birthday is the anniversary (day and month) of that date.

Somebody’ s date of birth is the date (day, month and year) when they were 405 birthday         a nd   date     of   birth For wide and broad, ►    412.

The river is 100 metres wide, (     .. . 400 metres large.) Large is a 'false friend’  for speakers of some languages. It does not mean 'wide’ .

4 large      and   wide the fixed expressions big business, big trouble.

Big and large are not generally used with uncountable nouns - but note He wrote a large part of the book while he was in prison.

She spent large sums  on entertaining.

somebody, or lend somebody something.

Lending (AmE  also loaning) is giving (for a time). You lend something to not I borrowed a pound from my brother, (     I borrowed-my-brother a-pound.) You borrow something from somebody.

Can I borrow your bicycle? (     Can I lend your-bieyele?) Borrowing is taking (for a time).

408 borrow        and   lend     _____________________ The king's body was borne away to the cathedral.

She bore six children in seven years. (More normal: She had six children ...) birth to’  and ‘ carry’ .

In a very formal style, bear can be used with other meanings, including ‘ give I can't bear her voice.

difficult experiences. It is most common in the expression can't bear (= hate, The  verb bear (bore, borne) is used to talk about accepting or tolerating 2 the   verb    bear My parents were born in Scotland.

I was born in 1936. (Nor Lam-bom-in-193&.) To  give a place or date of birth, we  use the simple past: was/ were born.

An informal word for a British person is Brit. Briton is used mainly in news England, and Scottish and Welsh people do not like to be called ‘ English’ .

Note that England is only one part of Britain. Scotland and Wales are not in round about.

Ireland and the Republic of Ireland, also called ‘ Eire’ ), and the smaller islands England, Scotland, Wales, the whole of Ireland (which includes both Northern The British Isles is a geographical, not a political, term. It is the name for some people also use Britain in this wider sense.
